title: Constructing Components and Other Objects
text: Constructing Components and Other Objects During the construction phase of application development activity, you create development objects that define the application components and runtime libraries needed for the application. Application components can reuse definitions that you created in the modeling phase. The high-level process for constructing application components is as follows: Create the required components. Uniface has different types of components suited to different purposes, such as Server Pages and Forms for the user interface and Services for business logic. For each component, define its data structure. This controls the data (component entities and fields) used in the component, the order in which data is processed, and the default behavior when it is stored or deleted. In most cases, component entities are created by inserting modeled entities. You can also create non-modeled component entities that are specific to the component. For more information, see Define the Component Structure . Write script modules that control component-specific behavior. For more information, see Script Modules . For presentation components, design the layout and appearance of the component. For server pages, this is done using HTML. Create global objects that components can access at runtime, such as messages, variables, and global ProcScript functions. Global objects are defined and deployed in libraries. The information in this section is applicable for all types of applications and components. Detailed information on developing specific types of applications is available in other sections. For more information, see Developing Web Applications and Developing Desktop Applications . Related Links Developing Web Applications You can use Uniface to develop, test, and deploy web-based applications. Web applications have unique requirements with regard to testing and deployment, state management and authentication, data maintenance, and security. Developing Desktop Applications Enterprise desktop applications are distributed client/server applications—they allow users to retrieve and maintain data in a remote database using a locally-run application. Desktop applications are usually highly interactive, and provide a much faster response time than most web applications. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
